title: Picrasma quassioides
intro: Picrasma quassioides, also known as Quassia, is a tropical tree species native to the Americas and the Caribbean, belonging to the family Simaroubaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its reported anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Quassia include quassin and nimbidin, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Quassia is often prepared as a tea or tincture, and it is also used in combination with other herbs to create remedies for various health conditions.
taxonomy: Picrasma quassioides belongs to the family Simaroubaceae, a small family of flowering plants. It is native to Southeast Asia and is often referred to as the Quassia tree due to its similarities in appearance to the Quassia amara tree. The classification of Picrasma quassioides is as follows: Kingdom - Plantae, Phylum - Magnoliophyta, Class - Magnoliopsida, Order - Sapindales, Family - Simaroubaceae, Genus - Picrasma, Species - P. quassioides. This plant is also sometimes classified as a member of the genus Quassia. Its exact classification may vary depending on the source consulted.
uses: Picrasma quassioides, also known as Quassia amara, has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. The plant contains compounds that have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, making it useful in treating various health conditions. In traditional medicine, Quassia amara has been used to treat fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Quassia amara for medicinal purposes.

constituents: Picrasma quassioides contains various active constituents, including quassin, picrasin, and quassimarin, which are responsible for its medicinal properties.
